data.title: Watch live: Artemis II crew holds press conference from space
data.description:
The Artemis II crew will give remarks from the Orion spacecraft Thursday evening, just days after the four astronauts traveled farther into space than anyone before them.

The crew — Cmdr. Reid Wiseman, pilot Victor Glover, mission specialist Christina Koch and Canadian Space Agency mission specialist Jeremy Hansen — embarked on the 10-day lunar flyby mission on April 1. The group, now on the way back to Earth, is expected to splash down in San Diego on Friday.

President Trump called the crew earlier this week to congratulate them on the mission. The conversation came after the astronauts viewed and recorded observations from the far side of the moon, a previously unseen surface.

“Everybody’s talking about this, and I look forward to having you in the Oval Office at the White House, and we will celebrate your incredible achievements and triumphs,” the president said.

Their comments are scheduled to begin at 6:10 p.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Trump administration proposes more flexible regulations for coal plants' toxic waste disposal
data.description:
The Trump administration is proposing to make regulations on the disposal of coal plants’ toxic waste more flexible.

When coal is burned to produce power, it results in a waste known as “coal ash” that contains contaminants including mercury and arsenic. Often coal ash is stored in ponds. Improper management of coal ash ponds can result in groundwater and drinking water pollution.

The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) on Thursday proposed giving permit authorities — either itself or the states — flexibility to allow plants to abide by “alternative groundwater protections standards” for certain pollutants. 

Its draft rule would also allow for groundwater monitoring to take place at “alternative” locations.

EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin said in a statement that the move would bolster the energy industry and hand more power to states. 

“These commonsense changes to the CCR regulations reflect EPA’s commitment to restoring American energy dominance, strengthening cooperative federalism, and accommodating unique circumstances at certain CCR facilities,” Zeldin said in a written statement.

However, environmental advocates said the changes could worsen public health and the environment. 

“Coal ash pollution has already cut too many American lives short. Our government should be strengthening safeguards against this toxic threat; instead, Donald Trump and Lee Zeldin are taking a sledgehammer to them,” Sierra Club senior attorney Bridget Lee said in a written statement. 

“We will fight back against this reckless and illegal move and work to deliver a healthier future for the people who drink the water and breathe the air near coal ash dumps,” Lee added.

data.title: Sotomayor says AI forecasting Supreme Court decisions a 'bad thing,' shows 'we're way too predictable'
data.description:
Supreme Court Justice Sonia Sotomayor on Thursday called artificial intelligence models that have found success in anticipating how the high court will rule in upcoming cases “a very bad thing.”

“It shows we’re way too predictable,” Sotomayor told students while speaking at the University of Alabama School of Law.

“And we may not be stepping out of our normal thinking and opening our minds to new ideas enough if something like an AI system can actually predict with that high a level of success what the outcome will be,” the senior liberal justice continued.

Sotomayor did not say which model she was specifically referring to, only indicating one of her colleagues had told her about it. But lawyers and advocates have advanced several models that purport to predict the court’s decisions with relatively high accuracy.

Sotomayor’s comments came in response to a question from a law professor who asked how she views the line between AI’s helpfulness and harm as the judiciary increasingly experiments with its capabilities.

“AI is a sophisticated human. It is — all of its input is input from human beings,” she said. “And because it is that, it has the potential to perpetuate the very best in us and the very worst in us.”

Still, Sotomayor acknowledged AI’s increasing prevalence in the courts. She recalled a recent dinner with some of her former law clerks, who now work at major law firms. They had told Sotomayor that all new associates at their firms are expected to use AI.

“I think for every student in this room, do not graduate this institution without learning how to master AI as a tool,” Sotomayor told the crowd.

The comments marked some of Sotomayor’s most extensive public remarks to date on AI. The justices have increasingly been weighing in. Chief Justice John Roberts dedicated his 2023 year-end report to the topic.

It’s also been creeping into some of the court’s actual cases. In March, the justices declined to take up an appeal concerning whether AI-created art could receive copyright protections without a human creator.

And last week, Justice Samuel Alito brought it up when the court was hearing a case being argued by a prominent AI advocate in the Supreme Court Bar.

“Just out of curiosity, do you think we should ask Claude to decide this case?” Alito asked him.

“No. I adhere to the wise judgment of this court,” the lawyer, Adam Unikowsky, responded.

data.title: Wisconsin mayor who removed ballot drop boxes won't face charges
data.description:
A Wisconsin mayor who removed an absentee ballot drop box from city hall ahead of the 2024 presidential election will not face charges, a special prosecutor said Wednesday.

Doug Diny, the mayor of Wausau, Wis., has maintained that he did nothing wrong when he carted away the city’s lone ballot drop box in September 2024, according to previous reporting from the The Associated Press. There were no ballots inside as the box had not been unlocked yet.

The outlet noted that the mayorship is a nonpartisan office, but Diny’s 2024 campaign had Republican support.

Fond du Lac County District Attorney Eric Toney, a Republican running for Wisconsin attorney general, was tapped in August 2025 to investigate the matter. He concluded on Wednesday that there was not enough evidence to prove Diny violated the law beyond a reasonable doubt.

He noted that that while state law prohibits breaking open or tampering with seals or locks on ballot boxes, it did not apply in this case because there were no ballots inside at the time, according to a statement obtained by WFRV.  

Toney also claimed the state would likely be unable to prove that Diny committed misconduct in public office or any other crimes because the box was intended to contain a mix of ballots and other materials for various city departments.

“There are some that wanted charges issued based on politics and others that did not want charges issued based on politics,” Toney said in a statement. “However, the facts are the facts and the law is the law, no matter how badly someone may wish for a result, our duty is to follow the facts, and the law, not politics or emotions.”

Diny defended his actions at the time by saying the box was not secured to the ground and that anyone could have stolen it, so he wanted to secure it in City Hall. He returned it a week later following concerns from the city’s elections clerk, according to the AP.

He acknowledged in a New York Times interview on Wednesday that the incident had been “to some level a national embarrassment” for the city of 40,000 people in central Wisconsin, but was pleased by the special prosecutor’s findings.

It was “time for the city to move with city business,” Diny told the newspaper.

The use of ballot boxes in Wisconsin, a key swing state, became a point of contention ahead of the 2024 election. The liberal state Supreme Court allowed officials to place drop boxes around their communities in July of that year, overturning its own precedent set two years earlier when the court was under conservative control.

President Trump has moved to crack down on mail-in voting in his second term, a practice he often claims contributed to widespread fraud in the 2020 presidential election despite a lack of evidence.

data.title: Van Hollen: Trump 'genocide' post shows Republican Party is 'bankrupt'
data.description:
Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.) on Wednesday went after the Republican response to President Trump’s recent post threatening Iran in which he stated that “a whole civilization will die.”

“It says something about just how bankrupt the Republican Party has become under Donald Trump, that none of them said a peep. And so, the reality is, the only thing, and the only thing they care about, is not the fact that the president of the United States threatened massive war crimes and genocide, the only thing they care about is their own elections,” Van Hollen told MS NOW’s Ali Velshi on “All In.”

“And, that means that if they’re looking at public sentiment, and they’re hearing that they’re going to get crushed in the midterm elections and there are lots of signs pointing in that direction. But they have to understand and be more afraid of their constituents than they are of Donald Trump,” he added.

In a Truth Social post Tuesday, Trump said that “a whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again” if Iran did not meet a deadline for a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

A swath of Democrats on Tuesday pushed to remove Trump from office due to his threats against Iran before the 8 p.m. EDT deadline. The president later announced a two-week ceasefire, which has since become tenuous.

“Donald Trump is openly threatening war crimes against the entirety of Iranian civilization. 25th Amendment proceedings must begin immediately, but if the Cabinet is too cowardly, the House should begin the impeachment process now,” Rep. Diana DeGette (D-Colo.) posted on the social platform X, referencing Trump’s “civilization” post.

Sen. Ed Markey (D-Mass.) pressed the House to start impeachment proceedings due to the post, labeling it “completely unstable and perilous.”

When reached for comment, White House spokesperson Anna Kelly said in an email to The Hill that, “as someone who enjoys sipping margaritas with terrorists, Chris Van Hollen has zero credibility on this topic or anything else.”

Kelly is referring to an incident last year when Van Hollen traveled to El Salvador to meet with Kilmar Abrego Garcia and the two men were photographed with what appeared to be margaritas in front of them. The Maryland senator later said that Salvadoran government officials placed glasses down on their table to make it look like they had been drinking.

data.title: Petraeus: Iran could emerge from war 'militarily weakened' but 'strategically strengthened'
data.description:
Retired Gen. David Petraeus, the former commander of U.S. Central Command, said there is a “paradoxical question” about the future of Iran amid its conflict with the U.S. and Israel.  

“We can only answer it when the ultimate resolution is achieved here, but that is whether Iran will emerge militarily very weakened,” the retired general told CNN’s Dana Bash. 

Petraeus said there is “no question” that Iran’s military has taken a hit from the joint U.S.-Israeli attacks. 

“But could they somehow, again paradoxically, emerge from this militarily weakened but perhaps actually strategically strengthened? And that revolves around what the ultimate resolution is of control of the Strait of Hormuz,” he said. 

The strait has been a major point of contention in the conflict, after Iranian counterstrikes on U.S. targets and energy infrastructure in the Gulf region effectively halted the flow of oil-carrying ships through the trade corridor. 

About 20 percent of the world’s oil consumption travels through the strait daily under typical conditions, and the effective halt in operations has hiked up global energy rates. 

Petraeus said that by “disrupting traffic,” Iran asserts control over the major trading corridor. The retired general cited an estimated backup of 700 vessels in the Gulf and said ship owners are waiting to have “absolute confidence” before authorizing a resumption of travel through the strait. 

After President Trump announced a two-week halt in fighting with Iran on Tuesday evening, ships slowly began traveling through the passage again. 

Iran has proposed a new toll on ships traveling through the strait of cryptocurrency equivalent of $1 per barrel of oil on board — a move backed by Trump. Petraeus warned of the ramifications of this new tax. 

“If it’s $200 million per vessel — which is reportedly what some companies have been paying to transit and you multiply that times 100 or more ships per day — that is a very substantial amount of hard currency to enable Iran to repair the extraordinary damage that has been done,” Petraeus said.

data.title: StubHub to pay customers $10M in settlement, FTC says: Do you qualify?
data.description:
Related video above: How loud can concerts get?

(NEXSTAR) – Ticket exchange and resale giant StubHub has agreed to pay $10 million to customers to settle a deceptive ticket pricing case, the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) said Thursday.

The consumer agency accused StubHub of breaking FTC rules designed to give concertgoers and sports fans the “all-in” price up front.

During mid-May of 2025, the FTC alleges that StubHub failed to show mandatory fees and charges, “including high-demand National Football League tickets in the lead-up to when the NFL schedule was announced on May 14, 2025.”

Christopher Mufarrige said in a news release that the FTC rules make it “very clear” that the total price of live-event tickets must be displayed upfront, and can’t be buried behind screens that a buyer would need to click through.

“Price transparency is essential to a free and competitive marketplace,” Mufarrige said. “Today’s settlement underscores the Commission’s commitment to ensuring that consumers pay the price they are promised.”

How will payments work?

An injunction filed in the Southern District of New York orders StubHub to make payments to eligible customers who bought tickets between May 12 and 14, 2025.

The order says StubHub must notify customers within 14 days, using the last known email address associated with the initial ticket purchase. If the address doesn’t work, the company is required to use any other address it might have for the customer.

Customers can expect the refund to arrive in the original form of payment, or via PayPal if the first method fails.

The payment will reimburse customers for the fees they paid (such as fulfillment and service fees). If the amount owed is more than the total in the consumer fund, however, the order states that everyone will receive a proportion of the amount owed.

A StubHub spokesperson told Nexstar in a statement:

“We have long supported all-in pricing because it provides clarity for fans. This settlement covers a limited number of transactions, spanning just three days in May 2025, where some listings on our site may have displayed ticket prices exclusive of fees. While we strongly disagree with the FTC’s view of the case, we are addressing their concerns by refunding a portion of those buyers’ fees.”

data.title: Khanna: Netanyahu in Situation Room 'a betrayal of the American people'
data.description:
Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) on Wednesday slammed Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for reportedly being in the White House’s Situation Room weeks before the U.S.-Israeli joint military operation in Iran began.

“Let’s start with this pledge,” Khanna, a possible 2028 White House contender, wrote on the social platform X. “The next Democratic President will never have the Israeli PM in the Situation Room. Trump having Netanyahu there before launching an immoral and illegal war is a betrayal of the American people.”

Khanna shared a screenshot of The New York Times‘ reporting about what led President Trump to choose to attack Iran. The story opens with Netanyahu’s visit to the White House on Feb. 11. The prime minister “was whisked inside with little ceremony, out of view of reporters, primed for one of the most high-stakes moments in his long career.”

The Times described Netanyahu entering a “highly classified presentation” with President Trump and other White House officials, and that the Situation Room was “rarely used for in-person meetings with foreign leaders.” The two leaders sat across from each other, instead of Trump sitting at the head of the table.

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

Khanna has long condemned Netanyahu, particularly over Israel’s war with Hamas in Gaza.

The California Democrat was one of several lawmakers in November 2025 to back a resolution recognizing Israel’s military campaign in the Gaza Strip as a genocide. Khanna also did not attend Netanyahu’s joint address to Congress in June 2024, and he voted against sending additional aid to Israel amid its attacks on Gaza.

“It was a hard vote,” he said on ABC’s “The Week” in April 2024. “I mean, look, it — this was a stance against a blank check for Netanyahu and offensive weapons unconditionally while he’s talking about going into Rafah with — when we know more women and children are going to die.”

The criticism toward Netanyahu comes amid the U.S. and Israeli agreement to a ceasefire deal in Iran that would bring hostilities to a halt and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Israel carried on with its strikes in Lebanon, however, despite Tehran’s condition that Israel and Gulf nations stop all attacks on Lebanon.

Netanyahu and the Trump administration initally denied that the ceasefire extended to Lebanon, a claim that Iranian officials and Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif contradicted. But Trump on Thursday directed Netanyahu to rein in Israel’s attacks on Lebanon, with the country having launched 100 airstrikes in 10 minutes on Wednesday.

The prime minister on Thursday told his cabinet to start negotiating with Lebanese officials “as soon as possible.”

“The negotiations will focus on disarming Hezbollah and establishing peace between Israel and Lebanon,” Netanyahu said, according to a statement obtained by NewsNation.

data.title: FAA eyes fines for American, Southwest over employee drug, alcohol testing violations
data.description:
American Airlines and Southwest Airlines could face fines of hundreds of thousands of dollars from the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) over allegations that the carriers violated drug- and alcohol-testing regulations for employees.

The FAA proposed a $255,000 civil penalty against American Airlines on Wednesday, accusing the company of allowing 12 flight attendants who tested positive for drugs and alcohol to resume “safety-sensitive duties” without fully completing required follow-up testing between May 2019 and December 2023.

“The employees tested positive for substances including alcohol, amphetamines, cocaine, marijuana, and methamphetamine,” the agency said in a news release. 

A spokesperson for American Airlines said the carrier is reviewing the FAA’s notice. 

“The safety of our customers and team members is paramount,” the spokesperson told The Hill in a statement. “We take drug and alcohol testing seriously and collaborate with the FAA to address any issues.

These programs have been enhanced over the years through meaningful action to strengthen procedures for oversight and accountability.”

The civil enforcement action came less than a week after the FAA accused Southwest Airlines of similar failures. 

The agency said on Friday that the carrier did not conduct all required follow-up testing for 11 employees, including pilots, flight attendants and aircraft mechanics, who tested positive for alcohol, marijuana, cocaine and amphetamines.

The instances involving Southwest occurred between August 2021 and July 2024, according to the FAA. The airline faces a $304,272 fine.

A Southwest Airlines spokesperson told The Hill in a statement that the carrier is engaging with the FAA and takes its alcohol and drug testing responsibilities seriously.

“We took immediate action more than two years ago to enhance our procedures and to strengthen oversight and accountability,” the spokesperson said. “Nothing is more important to Southwest than the Safety of our Customers and Employees.”

An FAA fine proposal serves as a formal notice that the agency intends to assess a monetary penalty for aviation regulation violations. Civil penalties can be appealed to an administrative law judge with the Transportation Department or the National Transportation Safety Board.

American and Southwest have 30 days to respond to the FAA enforcement letter.

data.title: Florida AG launches investigation into OpenAI, ChatGPT
data.description:
Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier (R) said Thursday he is opening a probe into OpenAI and its chatbot.

Uthmeier, in a video he posted online announcing the investigation, cited “concerns” over OpenAI data and technologies “falling into the hands” of the Chinese Communist Party and ChatGPT’s links to criminal behavior, including “child sexual abuse material, use by child predators and the encouragement by suicide and self-harm.”

The Florida attorney general also said he “learned” that the individual accused of fatally shooting two people at Florida State University last April was “assisted” by ChatGPT. 

An attorney for the family of Robert Morales, who was killed during the incident, said this week that the shooter “was in constant communication” with the chatbot leading up the shooting, according to the Tallahassee Democrat.

Morales, 57, and 45-year-old Tiru Chabba were killed in the shooting. 

Uthmeier said that subpoenas in his probe are “forthcoming,” and called on the Florida Legislature to pass legislation protecting children from the “dangers” of AI and to “further empower” his office to regulate the technology.

“The development and rollout of artificial intelligence (AI) is a monumental leap in technology,” Uthmeier said. “But it has not been without concern for public safety and national security.”

Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) has pushed for state-level regulations on AI, putting him at odds with President Trump. In December, Trump signed an executive order aimed at pre-empting state laws intended to regulate the technology in favor of federal standards.

Uthmeier said Thursday that AI “should exist to supplement, support and advance mankind, not lead to an existential crisis” or humanity’s demise.

“As Big Tech rolls out these technologies, they should not, they cannot put our safety and security at risk,” he added. “We support innovation, but that doesn’t given any company the right to endanger our children, facilitate criminal activity, empower America’s enemies or threaten our national security. 

“Companies that do so will be held accountable to the fullest extent.”

A spokesperson for OpenAI told The Hill Thursday that the company will “cooperate” with Uthmeier’s investigation. 

“Each week, more than 900 million people use ChatGPT to improve their daily lives through uses such as learning new skills or navigating complex healthcare system,” the spokesperson added. “Our ongoing safety work continues to play an important role in delivering these benefits to everyday people, as well as supporting scientific research and discovery. We build ChatGPT to understand people’s intent and respond in a safe and appropriate way, and we continue improving our technology.”
